text: The tumor is visually observed abutting the left portal vein at the porto-umbilical fissure and is near the left hepatic vein close to its origin.
The middle hepatic vein is visually identified as free from the tumor, serving as a landmark for the upcoming transection of the liver tissue.
The surgeon dissects the root of the left hepatic vein, carefully encircling it with umbilical tape for isolation.
The surgeon uses umbilical tape to sling the liver, providing stabilization and exposure for further dissection.
The surgeon positions the inferior end of the liver along the caudate groove, aligning it with the gallbladder fossa.
The liver sling is positioned to indicate the planned transection plane, providing a visual guide for the upcoming resection.
The surgeon follows the middle hepatic vein to divide a segment IV branch and the main left hepatic vein, using the vein as a landmark for precise transection.
The surgeon divides the left hepatic artery and the segment IV hepatic artery at the porta hepatis, using precise surgical techniques to separate these vessels.
The surgeon divides the left portal vein and the left hepatic duct, continuing the resection process in the liver.
